core_story.problem: Transforming this limited information into an accurate model of the dynamically changing 3D world re- mains an open research challenge, and progress in this space could enable applications in robotics, film-making, video games, and augmented reality.

Transforming this limited information into an accurate model of the dynamically changing 3D world re- mains an open research challenge, and progress in this space could enable applications in robotics, film-making, video games, and augmented reality. Abstract We present CAT4D, a method for creating 4D (dynamic 3D) scenes from monocular video. Abstract We present CAT4D, a method for creating 4D (dynamic 3D) scenes from monocular video. We demonstrate competitive performance on novel view synthesis and dynamic scene reconstruction benchmarks, and highlight the creative ca- pabilities for 4D scene generation from real or generated videos.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction While the world is a dynamic 3D environment, the images and videos we capture represent only a partial snapshot of this reality.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Introduction While the world is a dynamic 3D environment, the images and videos we capture represent only a partial snapshot of this reality. Transforming this limited information into an accurate model of the dynamically changing 3D world re- mains an open research challenge, and progress in this space could enable applications in robotics, film-making, video games, and augmented reality. Advances in 3D reconstruction have enabled the creation of accurate 3D and 4D models when certain careful capture requirements are followed [8,11, 14, 28, 35, 43, 46, 53, 72]. For static 3D reconstruction, this requires having a large set of multi-view consistent images of the 3D scene.

introduction_structure.main_message: This can be achieved through careful capture and restricted settings, which is challenging to accomplish in most environments, but often feasible with enough time and practice.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: This can be achieved through careful capture and restricted settings, which is challenging to accomplish in most environments, but often feasible with enough time and practice. Recon- structing 4D content, however, requires synchronized multi- view video, which is nearly impossible for a typical user. To reduce the dependence on these challenging capture requirements, several methods have turned to data-driven approaches leveraging learned 3D generative priors to im- prove the quality of 3D reconstruction [17,22, 58, 73]. Core to the success of these methods is the availability of high- quality multi-view image datasets that enable learning gen- erative priors.

introduction_structure.main_message: While this data exists in large quantities for static environments, the same cannot be said for 4D, where the complexity of building a synchronized multi-view cap- ture setup constrains both the amount and diversity of avail- able data, and therefore the ability to build a data-driven 4D prior.

Abstract We present CAT4D, a method for creating 4D (dynamic 3D) scenes from monocular video.
Advances in 3D reconstruction have enabled the creation of accurate 3D and 4D models when certain careful capture requirements are followed [8,11, 14, 28, 35, 43, 46, 53, 72].
CAT4D leverages a multi-view video diffusion model trained on a diverse com- bination of datasets to enable novel view synthesis at any specified camera poses and timestamps.

Combined with a novel sampling approach, this model can transform a sin- gle monocular video into a multi-view video, enabling ro- bust 4D reconstruction via optimization of a deformable 3D Gaussian representation.
Core to the success of these methods is the availability of high- quality multi-view image datasets that enable learning gen- erative priors.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version; the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
We present CAT4D, a method for creating 4D (dynamic
3D) scenes from monocular video. CAT4D leverages a
multi-view video diffusion model trained on a diverse com-
bination of datasets to enable novel view synthesis at any
specified camera poses and timestamps. Combined with a
novel sampling approach, this model can transform a sin-
gle monocular video into a multi-view video, enabling ro-
bust 4D reconstruction via optimization of a deformable
3D Gaussian representation. We demonstrate competitive
performance on novel view synthesis and dynamic scene
reconstruction benchmarks, and highlight the creative ca-
pabilities for 4D scene generation from real or generated
videos. See our project page for results and interactive de-
mos: cat-4d.github.io.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
While the world is a dynamic 3D environment, the images
and videos we capture represent only a partial snapshot of
this reality. Transforming this limited information into an
accurate model of the dynamically changing 3D world re-
mains an open research challenge, and progress in this space
could enable applications in robotics, film-making, video
games, and augmented reality.
Advances in 3D reconstruction have enabled the creation
of accurate 3D and 4D models when certain careful capture
requirements are followed [8,11, 14, 28, 35, 43, 46, 53, 72].
For static 3D reconstruction, this requires having a large set
of multi-view consistent images of the 3D scene. This can
be achieved through careful capture and restricted settings,
which is challenging to accomplish in most environments,
but often feasible with enough time and practice. Recon-
structing 4D content, however, requires synchronized multi-
view video, which is nearly impossible for a typical user.
To reduce the dependence on these challenging capture
requirements, several methods have turned to data-driven
approaches leveraging learned 3D generative priors to im-
prove the quality of 3D reconstruction [17,22, 58, 73]. Core
to the success of these methods is the availability of high-
quality multi-view image datasets that enable learning gen-
erative priors. While this data exists in large quantities for
static environments, the same cannot be said for 4D, where
the complexity of building a synchronized multi-view cap-
ture setup constrains both the amount and diversity of avail-
able data, and therefore the ability to build a data-driven
4D prior. Instead, methods have attempted to regularize
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
CAT4D is a two-stage approach for creating a dynamic 3D
scene from a monocular input video (see Fig. 4). First,
we transform the monocular video into a multi-view video
using a multi-view video diffusion model. Then, we re-
construct a dynamic 3D scene by optimizing a deformable
3D Gaussian representation from these generated multi-
view videos. We first describe our multi-view video dif-
fusion model (Sec. 3.1) and our carefully curated train-
ing datasets (Sec. 3.2). We then describe how our model
can be used to perform few-view “bullet-time” 3D re-
construction (Sec. 3.3). Lastly, we describe our sam-
pling strategy for generating nearly-consistent multi-view
videos (Sec. 3.4), and our reconstruction method that yields
exactly-consistent dynamic Gaussian models (Sec. 3.5).
